hero.kicker: For the visits insurance won't cover.
headline: Keep recovery going
when coverage stops.

heroLead: 22 Strong pays for hands-on recovery care for veterans and low-income youth when insurance runs out before the recovery is done.

mission.heading: Coverage runs out before recovery does.
mission.lead: The injury is real and the care plan is working. Then the next visit isn't covered. That gap is what we close.
needParagraphs: We confirm the gap, then pay the clinic for the sessions. Recovery keeps its schedule.

story.heading: The gap kept showing up in the treatment room.
paragraphs: 22 Strong started inside Next Level Fitness & Wellness Center, a sports-medicine clinic in Newport Beach co-owned by Travis Mayfield, a Purple Heart veteran of the 101st Airborne, and Dr. Vasili “Dr. Vas” Gatsinaris.

In the treatment room they kept seeing the same problem: the care was working, and the next visit wasn't guaranteed. They built 22 Strong so a recovery wouldn't depend on whether someone could afford one more appointment.

veterans.text: veterans report chronic pain after service. When pain is part of daily life, recovery care is not optional.
of post-9/11 veterans have not used VA health care since leaving the service.

athletes.text: U.S. children were uninsured in 2024, the highest rate in nearly a decade, and many more are underinsured. Families are left to cover recovery care on their own.
athletes.source: Georgetown University Center for Children and Families

athletes.text: U.S. families had trouble paying their child's medical bills last year. When recovery care isn't covered, the cost falls on the family.
athletes.source: KFF / National Survey of Children's Health, 2024

others.text: is the typical cost of one outpatient physical-therapy session for someone paying without insurance.
a year is a common insurance cap on physical therapy. Recovery often takes more.
of parents carry health care debt, far more than non-parents, and most of them have put off needed care because of the cost.

audiences.heading: Veterans
audiences.lead: Care funding for veterans rebuilding strength and mobility after service.
audiences.body: VA coverage and referrals get many veterans started. The plan often ends before the pain does. When that happens, 22 Strong pays for the sessions that finish the job.
audiences.photoAlt: Two veterans talking together at home

audiences.heading: Low-income youth
audiences.lead: Recovery care for young people whose families can't cover what it takes to heal.
audiences.body: An injured knee doesn't wait for a family's budget to catch up. When the next visit is out of reach, 22 Strong pays for it. That goes for student-athletes and any young person who needs hands-on care.
audiences.photoAlt: Youth basketball team huddling in a gym

featurePhoto.alt: Youth baseball players lined up along the dugout fence during a game

voices.name: Adam Folker
voices.quote: During my sophomore year in college, I had hip surgery that was potentially season-ending and even career-ending. My coaches referred me to Dr. Vas, who was able to take care of me and eventually get me back to playing within a short few weeks. That season I ended up winning hustle player of the year award.
voices.photoAlt: Adam Folker driving to the basket for UC Irvine

luke.story: Luke shares why access to hands-on recovery care matters after service.

kingMartin.story: King Martin shares his experience with 22 Strong Foundation's recovery care program.

veteranCare.story: A testimonial about veteran care and recovery support through 22 Strong Foundation.

adam.quote: During my sophomore year in college, I had hip surgery that was potentially season-ending and even career-ending. My coaches referred me to Dr. Vas, who was able to take care of me and eventually get me back to playing within a short few weeks. That season I ended up winning hustle player of the year award. Throughout my career, I saw Dr. Vas on various occasions for injuries that came up, along with maintenance and preventive care. I was able to play competitively at the top level.

veteranVoices.name: Tom Boscamp
veteranVoices.title: U.S. Army Veteran · Veteran Services Coordinator
veteranVoices.quote: I am a 65 year old Veteran who needs to be active since I am on the go taking care of Veterans in the Community. I have degenerative disk disease and Spinal Stenosis as well as radiculopathy. The VA treatments are far and few between as they do not have enough chiropractors, massage therapists, and Physical therapy specialists. Thankfully Dr. Vas has filled that void and with his treatments it has allowed me to continue being active. I have even lost 15 pounds! I can’t say enough how good Next Level Wellness is! Also their commitment to serving Veterans is “Next Level”!

strongCircle.heading: The 22 Strong Circle.
strongCircle.lead: A monthly membership that lets 22 Strong say yes to the next veteran or young person without waiting on the next fundraiser.
strongCircle.footnote: Monthly · cancel anytime · every dollar funds recovery care.

points.label: Care that continues
points.text: Funding that keeps going after the first visit.

points.label: Money we can plan on
points.text: Monthly gifts let us commit to a full care plan up front.

points.label: Veterans funding veterans
points.text: Neighbors keeping a neighbor's recovery going.

heading: A recovery gap appears.
22 Strong closes it.
howItWorks.kicker: Someone has a care plan and the next appointment is in doubt. Here is what happens next.

steps.heading: Referral or request
steps.body: Referrals come from the VA, veteran networks, coaches, school counselors, and local community organizations.

visual.alt: Athletic trainer wrapping an ankle after a sports injury
visual.label: A real injury becomes a request for help.

steps.heading: We confirm the gap
steps.body: 22 Strong reviews the situation. If coverage has run out or private care is out of reach, we step in.

steps.heading: Care funded
steps.body: 22 Strong pays the clinic directly. The patient never sees a bill.
steps.note: Care is currently provided through {provider} in Newport Beach, the sports-medicine clinic co-owned by 22 Strong's founders. Funded sessions can include Fascial Integrative Treatment (FIT) and hands-on care for rotator cuff, knee, neck, lower back, hip, ankle/foot, and wrist/elbow injuries.

disclosure.text: We publish how that provider relationship is managed.

outcome.heading: Care doesn’t stop.
outcome.body: The gap closes and the plan continues, visit after visit.

leadership.name: Dr. Vasili “Dr. Vas” Gatsinaris, D.C.
paragraphs: Dr. Vas earned his Doctor of Chiropractic from the Los Angeles College of Chiropractic in 2001 and has more than 25 years of experience helping people recover and rebuild.

Over his career, he has worked with everyone from professional athletes to first responders, developing a hands-on, integrative approach focused on restoring mobility and getting people back to life stronger and faster.
He is co-owner of Next Level in Newport Beach — the same recovery care 22 Strong funds.

leadership.name: Travis Mayfield
paragraphs: A Purple Heart recipient who served as a Sergeant with the U.S. Army’s 101st Airborne Division in Vietnam, Travis is Co-Owner and President of Next Level Fitness & Wellness Center. His own long road back from war is the inspiration behind 22 Strong’s model. He helped found the foundation and leads its mission to make recovery care accessible to those who served and to others rebuilding their lives.
His service and community work have been recognized by the California State Senate and Assembly, the U.S. House of Representatives, and Orange County.

governance.heading: The next layer belongs in public.
governance.lead: 22 Strong began with two founders and a working treatment room. Testimonials prove the care matters; governance has to prove the organization can outlast its founders. As board and advisory roles are formalized, that roster belongs here.

commitments.heading: Board and advisory roster
commitments.body: When board or advisory seats are formalized, 22 Strong will publish names, roles, and terms here so donors can see who shares oversight.

commitments.heading: Conflict standard
commitments.body: The Next Level relationship is named, linked, and governed by a disclosure rule: a conflicted leader should not approve a benefit to themselves or their business.

commitments.heading: Continuity path
commitments.body: The program is documenting referral review, funded-care records, and provider relationships so the model can keep operating as the organization grows.

donate.heading: Fund the next recovery appointment.
donate.lead: A one-time gift pays for hands-on care when the next visit is at risk.

storyImage.alt: Adaptive athlete deadlifting a loaded barbell
storyImage.caption: Recovery funding protects the long road from treatment to strength.

items.heading: Hands-on recovery sessions
items.body: Chiropractic, physical therapy, and massage therapy.

items.heading: Provider and partner referrals
items.body: A way in for veterans and families who are already trying to get well.
